item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is brilliant, as a huge comics fan I’ve always wondered why there weren’t more sequential art being used in skips… somehow I missed yours.

+1

item.title: Re: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Apologies. I wanted to put the two parts separate since the second part was less about the article itself and more of an extended discussion about one specific choice. I'll fix that now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well? I was bored one afternoon and had a keyboard, so I write long critiques. Why does anyone write extensively on this site?

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: MmePeignoir, instead of replying to your own comments, please edit your previous post using the "edit" function under the "options" tab to the lower right of every comment. That prevents spam buildup, and it's in the rules.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: anomalous webcomics should have a realistic amount of LGBT characters

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Been a while since I read this so I might be forgetting something, but I fail to see how statistics about LGBT people in the real world apply to this fictional webcomic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're getting yourself worked up with no possibility of a positive outcome (and several likely negative ones). I'd really recommend talking a walk or something. Get some fresh air. Reconsider why you feel the need to go on about this topic to such an extent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: [Removed due to double posting. Former contents now in parent comment]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Starts with an interesting and promising premise, but ultimately fails to deliver.

(Rather long review incoming)

+ show block
– hide block

The webcomic format certainly is an eye-grabber, but there is absolutely zero in-universe justification for this format screw. It's not an infohazard. The researchers could very easily type out a regular-old document, and add a link to the comic database at the end, and not confuse the hell out of new researchers in addition to looking extremely unprofessional - clearly the sensible thing to do here.

…Except it would be a pretty boring article that way. You see the problem here? When the only redeeming quality of the article is a format screw, and that format screw shouldn't even exist, clearly something is wrong.

Hell, now that I think about it, even if they decided to go with the webcomic format for the official documentation for whatever reason, they would've asked the characters to produce a new one, since this one consists of them babbling incoherently and going on rants halfway through the page before they even get to the containment procedures. There's just zero reason this thing should be on the actual database, ever.

Also, if the SCP is a webcomic, you would hope that at least it's a good webcomic. This is not a good webcomic. Neither is it parodying bad webcomic tropes in a meta, funny way - it's just straight up bad. The art is painfully mediocre and rather hard to read. There is no plot to speak of, not even a consistent world, which means that whatever arc they come up with is going to have a hard time leaving an emotional impact - we all know webcomics are made-up, but it's even harder to take them seriously if it's also made up in-universe, if even the characters admit they're basically making things up on the fly and doing elaborate role-play. I can suspend my disbelief, but not twice.

If I were Essie or Kahket, I'd shift the tone of the comic to comedy (lol).

Speaking of characters, there is very little characterization, and what little there is does not paint them as particularly likable people. In fact, most of them appear to be rather annoying - the wacky shenanigans type that are apparently incapable of taking anything seriously, as shown by the fact that they were asked to make the official documentation and spent the majority of the page doing nothing of the sort. In fact, the only character that I managed to like in any capacity is Kahnak, since he/she is apparently the only one who had the sense to actually do the job they were supposed to do (and yell at the others for not doing it - I'm not gonna deny it, reading that part was somewhat satisfying).

Clearly a lot of work was put into this, but it just doesn't manage to do anything that well. -1

There's also the matter of the whole ensemble being LGBT, which I'm going to put separately since it's somewhat off-topic.

+ show block
– hide block

About 4.5% of the American population self-identifies as LGBT[1]. Now, that study is based on self reporting, and apparently doesn't include asexuals or other minor sexualities, so I'm going to be generous and say about 8% is queer in one way or another.

So, what are the odds of six random people all being LGBT? Simple mathematics - 0.086=2.62*10-7, or 0.000000262. To put that into perspective, that's roughly equal to the odds of having 182 major characters, all of them being cishet.

That's not even taking into account that regular ol' lesbians and gay people make up the largest chunk of the LGBT population, while each of these six characters appear to be a different kind of queer. Lana is bi, Hope is presumably lesbian (since she's Lana's girlfriend and Word of God says no one else is bi), Jym is gay, Clyde is both trans and pan (because god forbid someone be straight amirite), and we don't know about Essie and Kahnak, but seeing the trend, I'm guessing one of them is probably ace (I might be wrong). Regardless, this makes this configuration actually happening spontaneously even less likely than the 2 out of ten million than I put out.

There are places where someone's sexuality can just be what it is and there doesn't have to be a reason (which is how it is in real life), and there are places where it's clearly the author making a political statement. I didn't really need to do the math to show this; the author even admitted in the comments that it was a political statement.

And seeing that they didn't even bother to put a "token straight" character in the bunch, I don't think this is a political statement that I'm going to particularly like. The whole thing is just too tumblr, and not in a good way.

(There might even be worse implications, considering the backstory of the skip. These characters are the only ones to wake up from a large ensemble from the original, non-anomalous webcomic. Presumably some of those were straight, but none woke up. Does this mean that the author believes straight characters are somehow less interesting? I don't know, and I don't want to assume malice where there might be none. It's certainly an unfortunate subtext, however.)

Citations: [1] https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-lgbt/some-4-5-percent-of-u-s-adults-identify-as-lgbt-study-idUSKCN1QM2L6

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You have to realize that the reason all the characters here are LGBT is because the author wanted all the characters to be LGBT. It's almost silly to complain that they don't follow real-world demographics when it's clear from the get-go that this is not meant to be a representation of the real world. Seriously, it's an anomalous webcomic, and these are fictional characters — not people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A format screw that has absolutely zero justification in-universe? No thanks bruh

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, no. These aren't a group of friends who banded up because of having shared experiences of being LGBT. These are just the characters from a much larger ensemble who just somehow ended up waking up - Lana used to be a villain, Jym is explicitly mentioned to be a one-off gag character, Kahnak is a DnD nerd and Clyde leads a band. Hope and Essie don't really have backstories mentioned, but seeing none of them actually mentioned how they knew each other from before they woke up, it's very likely that they didn't have significant ties back then.

So yeah, it's the equivalent of 6 random people somehow all being LGBT, which is statistically extremely unlikely, and therefore jarring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unique and slightly reminds me of ●●|●●●●●|●●|● with the article being told through images rather than a fledged out written article.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this SCP! From its premise alone it's already unique and it has that personal touch in that we not only see the subjects of the SCP have a personality but that they're cognisant of their situation. It ticks alot of the boxes I like for SCPs, an interesting and singular premise along with an execution which gives us enough information to mull over whilst not being entirely explained and deconstructed. It even has a mildly unsettling undertone which I always welcome in an entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a lot. The amount of creativity that authors put into their creations is mind-boggling nowadays. +1

edit: I used to be an idiot you know

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is no 'hook'

So the fact that the article's a webcomic just passes you by, huh

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"A generic bad webcomic, but it's an SCP" is not a bad idea. I just think that the first half or more of the article spends too much time being a bad webcomic to the point where I wanted to just skim through it to get to the interesting parts.

I guess it's ironic that I'm asking for the parody to be better than the thing it's parodying?

As for the people complaining about certain aspects of the cast… clearly they haven't seen enough bad webcomics. There were multiple comics that were essentially just filled with transformation fetishes; having people just happen to be a minority doesn't even register on the scale.

(all being supernatural creatures doesn't, either)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Every bad webcomic: the SCP edition. I do not see any redeeming qualities in this. There is no 'hook' that is an integral part of SCPs. Unoriginal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You can edit your posts.
Edit: Also, I agree. Knowing about the alt text definitely improves my opinion of this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the end I went with a +1 as I like the concept and I think a lot of people in the comments have been unduly harsh. The art is bad and a bunch of other stuff alone irks me, but together it does feel like an authentic web comic someone would have sincerely posted online 15 years ago. I definitely read this comic for like 6 months before I forgot about it.

There’s the obvious criticisms which I agree with; the art is woeful if in keeping with what it’s going for, the containment panel is also bad even if, again, early 2000s webcomics did that, and there are too many characters not doing much and there’s no reason most of this couldn’t just be an article that says the scp is contained on this one page so it doesn’t inadvertently become neutralised.

The biggest and most obvious question with the scp is what happened to the in fiction author, they just abandoned this comic or something? What happened to them? I feel like that might have helped make for a bigger sting than leaning on the tulpa effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a pretty decent format screw and a good dose of creativity. I enjoyed it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Weekly is better than monthly, right?
From what I heard, it is better to withhold content for droughts, to keep a steady flow of content up. What shape would the comic updates take?
I doubt that the mods would be happy with single picture articles.
If you are doing it, however you are doing it, I would be pretty excited.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would LIKE to continue it, but I'm trying to work out a schedule for doing this so I can make comics without feeling overworked but also not have people lose interest. I was thinking maybe weekly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I have commented on a few of the comments here, more than usual.

I like this thing and would love to see more of it.

My first online community was DeviantArt and it is a very soft place. I made two accounts over the years, and both times at least one person came to greet me. Every thing I made was noticed by somebody, and I learned how good that feels, to be acknowledged for one's own creation.
Because of that, I myself started "acknowledging" other people's creations. Either in the form of praise or constructive criticism.

I have also dabbled in webcomics, and learned of the most dreadful thing that can happen to one.
Hiatus because the art takes too much work.

Most of the good webcomics out there are dead corpses in very pretty graves.
It is why I don't know what will happen to the guy in broodhollow,
why that one mermaids thing which was rebooted before even starting has actually not even done that.

Many people have existing talents, jump into webcomics, since they cause a constant demand for the talents, lest they stagnate and realize that they will be 80 at chapter ten.

That is part of the reason, why I have been so active in this thread.
Another part is, that this is the perfect first chapter.
It quickly introduces the characters, gives us a quick tour of a part of the world, subverts some things and then punches us in the face with a mood switch.

I am not too interested in SM, but I love me some punches with mood switches.

TLDR:

I absolutely love the idea of a meta scp webcomics, which is also a scp.
I want to leave my +1 here and ask: Will this continue?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have seen and enjoyed far worse.
Read an entire furry comic and I don't even like curries(i love curry, autocorrect hates writing furries), because the story captured me.

In my opinion, the purpose of a comic is to draw a situation in a story, the story itself and do it all in a reasonable timeframe.
Since story and timeframe fit, this is a good comic with two of three points.

If this was at the level of asterix and obelix, with 200 strips, it would be a worse thing.

If you ever stumbled upon "one punch man" you may have heard about the original author.
That art is some of the worst I have ever see.
It is also one of the few mangas, I have read from beginning to end (at that time) within a month.

If someone wonders, why I seem so invested in this, it is because I really love the idea and hope to see more of this. I may comment separately concerning that matter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe it was mentioned in case anyone takes jym up on the whole idea of writing fan fiction. After all, it was only in the author comment.

I absolutely get what you are saying, and at first was writing something defending your point, but the whole point of the last strips is to say:"we need fan fiction please help us we are dying."
Since this is a site full of writers, it can be expected that people will take the challenge.

Makes sense to make some things clear outside of the story, as to not clutter the story itself "just" to have accurate fan fiction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My guess? Jym is a side character, originally a one off gag.
Unimportant and not supposed to turn up later.
Suggests among other things, to write smut with them and another character, but doesn't care either way.

Probably asexual and so on.

But that is only if character traits are set in stone from the point of origin for them.
If not, it could be literally anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, and I have to commend the amount of effort that went into this, but I dislike it because it's painful to read.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm revisiting this article after a few days right now - there are things better and worse than I originally thought.

The good: I didn't notice the mouseover alt-text at first, which really both gives it an authentic webcomic feel, and adds another layer to the Daesil storyline. Daesil has a wistful sadness that comes through the simple mouseover texts, which is nicely done.

The bad: There was really not enough of a strong reasoning or story - I later realized that, with what Jym said in the final panels, that the SCP relies on a existence sort of like SCP-1055 or SCP-2547, where knowledge/appreciation gives its strength - it's done differently enough, but it also wasn't elaborated fully upon. There's an awful lot of text in this for a webcomic, and half of it was conversational crap that did not do much in way of characterization, explanation, or advancing a story.

I know it would take a lot of effort - but I would really, really encourage you to revise the storyline. I know the format of it being a comic would make this extremely tedious, but I still think the biggest weakness of this is the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The way this entire article is formatted hurts my eyes. The speech bubbles are so poorly worked in to this that it feels like they where drawn by an eight year old with a pencil.

The way this piece is structured makes it feel very out of place on the SCP wiki, since the way that the typical SCP format is represented feels like an afterthought, causing the containment procedures to feel very tacked on.

The art isn't all that great either. The proportions on the character's feel off, and there's way to many panels full of exposition.

Overall a solid -1 for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: also wow there are a lot of people who are upset about lgbt people existing but who totally arent queerphobic at all in this comments section lmao

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: imo the artstyle is what makes this, its a v authentic bad oughties comic look

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Irrelevant to your previous claim that

So an oversaturated style is the only… style? I mean I can draw like Frank Miller. That doesn't mean I would convey the same impactful punch he did given he setup his niche so firmly in history by this point. My point is that further attempts to ape at styles that already exist because someone pioneered them is detrimental to an overall product if you can't find your own voice.

Your style isn't your own or your style will get seen as derivative. More importantly, your style may not even fit with the environment SCP articles convey (most of the time). Going off webcomic styles, we're talking a huge wide swathe of genres here. From political to fantastical. That makes this SCP even less justified for existing.

Being LGBT is this.

I respectfully disagree. Nor do I think this should be a defining attribute. I don't watch YourMovieSucks or read Langston Hughes (supposedly bi or gay from some accounts) for that specific attribute alone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because fans often ignore canon sexualities

Though in the one suggestion it listed Jym and Cayde who are gay and pan respectively so that pairing could be canon anyway

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pudding, as much as I appreciate you jumping to my defense about this, they’re not all bisexual, only Lana is.

For how long? It literally instructs the containment team to write about them all fucking each other.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pudding, as much as I appreciate you jumping to my defense about this, they’re not all bisexual, only Lana is.

They’re all LGBT because

1. This comic is supposed to play on tropes of poorly-made 00s webcomics, and what sounds more webcomic-y than “Gay Supernatural High School Teens”

2. I wanted them to be because representation is important no matter how small and no matter where it is

It’s more along the vein of what DrMagnus said, there’s just no reason why they SHOULDN’T be LGBT

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe Desai was straight…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also a valid point, above. Being able to ship with any character would potentially increase drama, and thus readership.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: True, but ironically in this case there is a good survival motive for them to all be bisexual.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As my art teacher once said to me, just because anime is popular anon, doesn't mean your work specifically is better. Nor is it suitable for learning more on art theory before progressing into your own style.

Irrelevant to your previous claim that "this sort of art-style would never be appreciated anywhere outside of a blog or a tumblr-post with a few select fans", which is demonstratively false.

This isn't substantively different from comics that are widely appreciated, but probably more relevant to plausibility also very common. There's literally only one Dresden Codak, one Stand Still Stay Silent, one Girl Genius ect. That this looks a lot like the modal webcomic instead is, IMO, a feature here.

I could argue adapting to recent trends in comics on a marketplace aggregate like say for webcomics instead of Marvel or DC would be highly valued by the sentient comic entities then.

Being LGBT is this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
- No actual reason why the entire enclave had to be LGBT. Instead of making something interesting that alludes to the nature of the author, it falters really hard. If we are to assume the characters represent facets of the author, then she is one-dimensional. Though personality quirks exist, nothing impresses upon me anything of value in the long-term.

There's no reason why they shouldn't be either. You don't need a "reason" to have your characters identify a certain way. Just like you don't need a "reason" to be LGBT in regular life. It's just part of who they are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
US Alexa rank of 7,326

As my art teacher once said to me, just because anime is popular anon, doesn't mean your work specifically is better. Nor is it suitable for learning more on art theory before progressing into your own style.

That interlude aside… this simplistic art style is too saturated for me to take seriously or contemplate further on. I mean no offense to the hard work that was poured into it. Just not for me.

being able to ship with each other…

Eh. That honestly makes this concept worse overall. I could argue adapting to recent trends in comics on a marketplace aggregate like say for webcomics instead of Marvel or DC would be highly valued by the sentient comic entities then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Inexperienced designs lead me to believe this sort of art-style would never be appreciated anywhere outside of a blog or a tumblr-post with a few select fans.

The first strip of a webcomic with a US Alexa rank of 7,326, thousands of books sold, spin-off board games and tabletop RPGs, and now on strip 6,605:
https://www.schlockmercenary.com/2000-06-12

Also, literally most of Keenspot looks like this.

- No actual reason why the entire enclave had to be LGBT.

Survival. They said it themselves, fanfic keeps them alive. Being able to ship with each other is adaptive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Things I appreciated:

- black highlights expressing the internalization of your chars.
- clear personality tropes.
- a bold take on format screw.

Things that irked me:

- poor quality in artistry. Inexperienced designs lead me to believe this sort of art-style would never be appreciated anywhere outside of a blog or a tumblr-post with a few select fans.
- does nothing that other format-screws couldn't have accomplished better.
- diminishes the lasting horror / flash-fiction nature of SCP.
- logically, this thing could be contained by researchers and explained by them as being sentient comic panels. No actual reason provided for this format to exist.
- I get the metaphor of the starving artist. That to live, one must express oneself to the cruelty and ambivalence of someone else's POV. But you don't give the reader time to breath. Time for it to sink in, imo.
- grievous crime of exposition.

- No actual reason why the entire enclave had to be LGBT. Instead of making something interesting that alludes to the nature of the author, it falters really hard. If we are to assume the characters represent facets of the author, then she is one-dimensional. Though personality quirks exist, nothing impresses upon me anything of value in the long-term.

- disregarding that previous criticism, if the author (irl) who formatted the SCP article really did mean for these characters to just come out of nowhere with no background nor allusion, all you have then are dull characters. Really, dull characters.
- finally, the vague threat made by the pixie in a jersey is very overt. Feels like some rushed out implementation to justify this thing existing as a threat.

It's a downvote. Good premise, very poor execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
True, but I did not mean to imply this.

To me, saying "Oh, this guy is gay" is just as out of place as saying "Oh, this guy is straight". My assumption is that the author us giving me this info for a reason. After all, it's important enough to mention, right? So if the point is dropped there with no further impact in the story, it's kinda hanging out there like a sore thumb. Why was it so important to get mentioned if it's never really used or expanded on?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not to be accusative, but it's awfully heteronormative to say that an LGBT character's sexuality needs to have a "real impact" on the story/interactions/characterization, when such standards are never applied to straight/cis characters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A character's sexuality or gender doesn't NEED to add anything to the story. It only comes up like, twice in the article anyway.

So why must the mention be made at all if the character's sexuality or gender identity is that irrelevant to the overall story?

There's no specific "need" for a character to be one thing or another, but to mention it and kinda let it hang there without any real impact to the story or any intercharacter interactions (or worse, even the characterization of said character) that are shown, that's typically seen as lazy writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP has problems imo, but the characters being LGBT literally does not impact its quality at all? A character's sexuality or gender doesn't NEED to add anything to the story. It only comes up like, twice in the article anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay I just realized that this had alt-text, and the alt-text on the last comic is actually kinda killer. Props for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They are saying that their orientation does not matter and add nothing to the story, not that they need to be this or that. It is just an irrelevant detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do characters need a reason to be straight?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: upvoted for the work and the guts it took to post this. I appreciate a lot about what this comic was trying to do, but it's hard to feel an emotional connection to "the characters of a bad webcomic come to life, and try to deal with that" (which is a real strong concept) when it's told via… a bad webcomic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: you don't think the supernatural creatures - an orc, a zombie, etc - are anomalous enough?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is interesting as a format screw. However, i dislike the story. If it is some story about the life anomalous creature, It should have a surreal and plainly weird stuff. Or people living in the anomalous community, such are anartist or anomalous merchant. An easy for me example is a sentient carpet. But, i appreciate your work. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't have a problem with people who enjoyed the SCP for what it is. However, as much as there may be precedent for this in other forms of media, I find the intentional creation of bad art significantly more displeasing than a sincere effort to make a good webcomic, regardless of whether that was successful or not (although my opinion may not matter, as I had already downvoted). This looks bizarrely like actively celebrating the posting of low-quality work to the site for whatever reason and attempting to find an after-the-fact justification.

My response to your final sentence would be that things that look like low art, talk like low art and think like low art purely being 'low art' with no further substance are about ten thousand times more common than high art disguised as the same and if you present your art as 'low' (though I wouldn't use these words myself) expect it to be treated as such.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This touches my heart to hear you say this

It means a lot to have a person put into words what I was trying to get across, and it means even more that it was the author of some of my favorite articles on this website.

I truly appreciate everything you have to say here, thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know what this is now.

It's the
Down with Love of SCPs.

A spot on, loving, intelligent and well crafted parody of something not a lot of people get or know about and was kind of shitty to begin with. Compare bad webcomics to Doris Day/Rock Hudson movies.

This so closely mimics the tropes of bad webcomics while also intelligently creating a layered story with an intriguing structure and some nice, very gentle commentary. But it so closely mimics the sources material that the pitfalls of the original subject taint the pastiche. The bad, rushed art, clunky dialogue, wasted panel space and bland story ring very true to the world of webcomics, and have been meticulously presented.

Another example would be something like The Exorcist or Hereditary basically using the visual and written language of cheap horror films to tell high family drama, or the films of John Waters embracing a kitsch, grotesque aesthetic from 1950s American pop culture while having serious things to say about society.

Just remember, if it looks like low art, talks like low art, thinks like low art, but was made as high art, it just might be high art.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not the best execution, but I gave it a +1 for the concept.

Perhaps in the months or years to come this SCP will get a rewrite of sorts, but for now I think it's fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love what this is trying to do, and I agree with CC that it's great to have people trying new things with this format.

I tend to dislike webcomics, and the traits I attribute to that are on display here - mainly, the focus on slice-of-life details about more or less ordinary emotions and relationships. Stories about dating within friend groups do nothing for me, but I realize that's a matter of taste rather than quality so I'm not holding it against this. In fact, the themes and the dialogue and even the character design all seem very authentic to me; based on my limited experience with webcomics, this looks like a real one.

I can evaluate this better in terms of how it does as an SCP; thematic "webcomicness" aside, the pacing seems off. Sapient characters trapped inside pieces of media is, as others have pointed out, well explored territory already, so getting into the nitty-gritty of such a character's experience makes sense as a way to mine something new from the premise. For that reason, I think this would benefit from some tightening up in the character department; they pretty much all blurred together for me, and it felt like all those introductions left barely any room for getting into the existential horror of their situation. I think this would hypothetically make a better read if you were to cut one of the couples altogether (take your pick of which) and use the freed space to go deeper into the experiences of the ones that are left.

Even though I ended up at a novote, I'm really impressed by the effort that went into this and I appreciate the novelty of it, and would definitely be excited to see more of this kind of experimentation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I like that it looks a lot like the modal webcomic.

Agreed. This feels like your standard webcomic, one among thousands out there that, under normal circumstances, probably wouldn't ever be famous or amount to much.

Makes the anomaly feel more "real" IMO.
